examples.title: EOF Detection using get() and stream bool
examples.description: Demonstrates character-by-character reading with EOF detection using stream's bool operator
examples.explanation: The while(infile.get(ch)) pattern works because operator bool returns !fail(). When EOF is reached, get() fails and the loop terminates. This is the recommended way to read until EOF.

examples.title: EOF Detection using getline()
examples.description: Line-by-line file reading until EOF using std::getline()
examples.explanation: std::getline() reads each line and returns the stream object. The bool conversion returns false when EOF is reached, making it ideal for line-by-line processing.

examples.title: Binary File EOF Detection
examples.description: Reading binary file with proper EOF detection using read() and gcount()
examples.explanation: For binary files, read() returns false when it cannot read the full buffer size. gcount() tells how many bytes were actually read. This handles partial reads at EOF correctly.

examples.title: Multiple EOF Detection Methods
examples.description: Demonstrates different EOF detection approaches with comparison
examples.explanation: Shows three approaches: eof() flag check (not recommended), stream bool (recommended), and peek() for look-ahead. Method 2 is most reliable as it checks stream state directly.

examples.title: Error Handling with Stream States
examples.description: Complete example showing proper handling of different stream states
examples.explanation: Demonstrates how to check stream state after operations. Shows the difference between eof(), fail(), bad() and how clear() enables repositioning to read the file again.
